expanded_description: The Heckscher-Ohlin (H-O) model, developed by Eli Heckscher (1919) and Bertil Ohlin (1933), is a general equilibrium model of international trade that explains trade patterns based on differences in countries' factor endowments (relative abundances of labor, capital, land, and other productive factors). Unlike the Ricardian model, which attributes trade to differences in technology (labor productivity), the H-O model assumes identical technologies across countries and explains trade by differences in factor endowments. A country will export goods that intensively use its abundant factors and import goods that intensively use its scarce factors. The model generates several important theorems: the Heckscher-Ohlin theorem (trade pattern), the Stolper-Samuelson theorem (effects on factor prices), the Rybczynski theorem (effects of factor accumulation), and the factor price equalization theorem (long-run convergence of factor prices across countries). The H-O model provides a more complete explanation of trade patterns between countries with similar technologies but different factor endowments (e.g., North-South trade, trade between developed and developing countries). Understanding the Heckscher-Ohlin model is essential for analyzing how trade affects income distribution, how factor accumulation affects trade patterns, and whether globalization leads to convergence of wages and returns to capital across countries.

core_concept.principle:
The Heckscher-Ohlin model is a general equilibrium trade model with the following key assumptions: Two countries (Home and Foreign), two goods (e.g., manufactured goods and agricultural goods), two factors of production (labor $L$ and capital $K$). Factors are perfectly mobile between sectors within a country but immobile between countries. Production technologies are identical across countries (same production functions), but technologies differ across goods (one good is labor-intensive, the other capital-intensive). Countries differ in their factor endowments: one country is relatively labor-abundant, the other capital-abundant. Preferences are identical and homothetic across countries. Perfect competition prevails in all markets. There are no trade barriers or transport costs.

The Heckscher-Ohlin theorem: A country will export the good that intensively uses its abundant factor and import the good that intensively uses its scarce factor. If Home is labor-abundant ($L/K > L^*/K^*$) and manufacturing is labor-intensive ($L_M/K_M > L_A/K_A$), then Home will export manufacturing and import agriculture.

The Stolper-Samuelson theorem: An increase in the relative price of a good raises the real return to the factor used intensively in that good and lowers the real return to the other factor. Thus, trade (which raises the price of the export good and lowers the price of the import good) benefits the owners of the abundant factor and harms the owners of the scarce factor. This theorem provides a stark prediction about income distribution: in a labor-abundant country, workers gain from trade, capitalists lose; in a capital-abundant country, capitalists gain, workers lose.

The Rybczynski theorem: At constant prices, an increase in the endowment of a factor increases the output of the good that uses that factor intensively and decreases the output of the other good. This theorem explains how factor accumulation (e.g., population growth, capital investment) affects production patterns and trade.

The Factor Price Equalization (FPE) theorem: Under the assumptions of the H-O model, free trade leads to equalization of factor prices (wages and returns to capital) across countries, even if factors are immobile internationally. Trade in goods substitutes for factor mobility, driving wages and capital returns to equality.

The H-O model provides a powerful framework for understanding how trade affects factor prices, income distribution, and production patterns. However, empirical tests (the Leontief paradox) have shown that the simple H-O model does not always match real-world trade patterns, leading to extensions incorporating human capital, technology differences, and economies of scale.
core_concept.explanation:
Consider two countries: Home (labor-abundant) and Foreign (capital-abundant). Two goods: manufacturing (labor-intensive) and agriculture (capital-intensive). Home has more labor per unit of capital than Foreign: $L/K > L^*/K^*$. Manufacturing requires more labor per unit of capital than agriculture: $L_M/K_M > L_A/K_A$. According to the H-O theorem, Home will export manufacturing (the labor-intensive good) and import agriculture (the capital-intensive good). Why? Because manufacturing is relatively cheaper in the labor-abundant country (labor is cheaper), and agriculture is relatively cheaper in the capital-abundant country (capital is cheaper).

Now consider the effects of trade on factor prices. Before trade, in the labor-abundant country, labor is relatively cheap (low wage) and capital is relatively expensive (high return). In the capital-abundant country, capital is cheap and labor is expensive. When trade opens, the price of the labor-intensive good (manufacturing) rises in the labor-abundant country (because it can now export it at higher world prices). The Stolper-Samuelson theorem says that this price increase raises the real wage (return to labor, the abundant factor) and lowers the real return to capital (the scarce factor). In the capital-abundant country, the price of the capital-intensive good (agriculture) rises, raising the return to capital and lowering the wage. Trade benefits the abundant factor in each country and harms the scarce factor.

This is a stark and controversial prediction: trade benefits workers in labor-abundant developing countries (which is consistent with evidence of rising wages in China, Vietnam, Bangladesh) but harms workers in capital-abundant developed countries (which is consistent with evidence of wage stagnation or inequality in the US and Europe). The H-O model thus provides a theoretical foundation for the argument that trade contributes to rising inequality in developed countries.

The Rybczynski theorem explains how factor accumulation affects production. If Home's labor supply grows (e.g., through population growth or immigration), output of the labor-intensive good (manufacturing) increases, and output of the capital-intensive good (agriculture) decreases (at constant prices). This is called the 'Rybczynski effect' and explains patterns of structural transformation as economies develop.

The Factor Price Equalization theorem suggests that free trade should equalize wages and capital returns across countries over time. This is because trade in goods indirectly trades the factors embodied in those goods. If goods can move freely, the prices of the factors used to produce them should converge. Empirically, there has been some convergence in wages between developed and developing countries, but full equalization has not occurred due to technology differences, trade barriers, and factor immobility.
The Heckscher-Ohlin model is a workhorse of international trade theory, but it has limitations. The Leontief paradox (1953) found that the US — a capital-abundant country — exported labor-intensive goods and imported capital-intensive goods, contradicting the H-O theorem. Subsequent research resolved the paradox by incorporating human capital (skilled labor as a form of capital), technology differences, and product differentiation. Despite its limitations, the H-O model remains essential for understanding how factor endowments shape trade patterns and how trade affects income distribution.

image.alt: Diagram showing two countries with different factor endowments (labor-abundant vs capital-abundant) and two goods with different factor intensities (labor-intensive vs capital-intensive)
image.caption: Heckscher-Ohlin model: Countries export goods that intensively use their abundant factors. Trade affects factor returns (Stolper-Samuelson) and production patterns (Rybczynski).

sections.introduction: The Heckscher-Ohlin (H-O) model explains international trade patterns based on differences in countries' factor endowments — their relative supplies of labor, capital, land, and other productive factors. Unlike the Ricardian model, which emphasizes technology differences, the H-O model assumes identical technologies and focuses on factor abundance. The model generates powerful predictions about trade patterns, factor prices, income distribution, and the effects of factor accumulation.

key_concept.definition:
The Heckscher-Ohlin model is a general equilibrium trade model with the following structure: Two countries (Home and Foreign), two goods (Good 1 and Good 2), two factors of production (labor $L$ and capital $K$). Factors are perfectly mobile between sectors within a country but immobile between countries. Production technologies are identical across countries (same production functions), but factor intensities differ across goods. One good is labor-intensive ($L_1/K_1 > L_2/K_2$), the other capital-intensive. Countries differ in factor endowments: one country is relatively labor-abundant ($L/K > L^*/K^*$), the other capital-abundant. Preferences are identical and homothetic across countries. Perfect competition prevails. No trade barriers or transport costs.

Key theorems of the H-O model: Heckscher-Ohlin theorem (trade pattern): A country exports the good that uses its abundant factor intensively and imports the good that uses its scarce factor intensively. Stolper-Samuelson theorem (factor prices): An increase in the relative price of a good raises the real return to the factor used intensively in that good and lowers the real return to the other factor. Rybczynski theorem (output effects): At constant prices, an increase in the endowment of a factor increases the output of the good that uses that factor intensively and decreases the output of the other good. Factor Price Equalization (FPE) theorem: Under the assumptions of the H-O model, free trade leads to equalization of factor prices across countries.

The H-O model provides a framework for understanding North-South trade (developed vs developing countries), the effects of globalization on income inequality, and the relationship between factor accumulation and structural transformation.
key_concept.context: The Heckscher-Ohlin model was developed by Swedish economists Eli Heckscher (1919) and Bertil Ohlin (1933). Ohlin received the Nobel Prize in Economics in 1977 for this work. The model was the dominant theory of international trade from the 1930s through the 1960s, until the Leontief paradox (1953) challenged its empirical validity. Subsequent extensions incorporated human capital, technology differences, and product differentiation. Despite its limitations, the H-O model remains a cornerstone of trade theory and is taught in every advanced undergraduate and graduate trade course. The Stolper-Samuelson theorem, in particular, is central to debates about trade and income inequality.

categories.description: The key assumptions that define the H-O framework.

examples.process: Two countries, two goods, two factors (2x2x2)
examples.explanation:
The simplest H-O model has two countries (Home and Foreign), two goods (e.g., manufacturing and agriculture), and two factors (labor and capital). This minimal structure allows for general equilibrium analysis while remaining tractable.

Example: Home (US) and Foreign (Mexico). Goods: computers (capital-intensive) and textiles (labor-intensive). Factors: capital and labor. The US is capital-abundant ($K/L > K^*/L^*$); Mexico is labor-abundant ($L/K > L^*/K^*$).

Unlike the Ricardian model, the H-O model assumes that production functions are the same in both countries. Differences in factor endowments, not technology, drive trade.

Example: The same production function for textiles applies in both the US and Mexico. Differences in factor costs (wages, capital returns) lead to different autarky prices, creating trade incentives.

examples.process: Perfect factor mobility within countries, immobility between countries
examples.explanation:
Labor and capital can move freely between sectors within a country (e.g., from agriculture to manufacturing) but cannot move between countries. This assumption contrasts with the specific factors model (where some factors are immobile) and with models of international factor mobility.

Example: A textile worker in Mexico can move to the computer sector (if wages are higher), but cannot move to the US to work.

Consumers in both countries have the same preferences, and preferences are homothetic (income elasticities = 1). This ensures that differences in demand do not drive trade; only supply-side differences matter.

Example: Both US and Mexican consumers have Cobb-Douglas preferences $U = C_c^{0.3} C_t^{0.7}$. The share of income spent on computers (30%) is the same in both countries regardless of income level.

examples.process: Perfect competition and no trade barriers
examples.explanation:
All markets are perfectly competitive. There are no tariffs, quotas, subsidies, or transport costs. These assumptions ensure that prices equal marginal costs and that the law of one price holds for traded goods.

Example: The price of textiles in Mexico equals the world price (adjusted for exchange rates) after trade liberalization.

categories.description: Defining and measuring factor abundance and factor intensity.

examples.explanation:
A country is relatively abundant in a factor if its ratio of that factor to the other factor is higher than in the other country. Home is labor-abundant if $L/K > L^*/K^*$. Alternatively, Home is capital-abundant if $K/L > K^*/L^*$.

Example: Home has 100 units of labor and 50 units of capital ($L/K=2$). Foreign has 50 units of labor and 50 units of capital ($L/K=1$). Home is labor-abundant ($2 > 1$).

A country is relatively abundant in a factor if the autarky price (return) of that factor is lower than in the other country. The physical and price definitions are equivalent under the assumptions of the H-O model.

Example: In labor-abundant Home, the autarky wage $w$ is lower than in Foreign; the autarky return to capital $r$ is higher than in Foreign. $w/r$ is lower in Home.

A good is labor-intensive if it uses a higher ratio of labor to capital than the other good: $L_1/K_1 > L_2/K_2$. Factor intensity is a technological relationship that is assumed to be the same across countries and does not change with factor prices (no factor intensity reversals).

Example: Manufacturing uses 2 units of labor and 1 unit of capital per unit output ($L/K=2$). Agriculture uses 1 unit of labor and 2 units of capital per unit output ($L/K=0.5$). Manufacturing is labor-intensive; agriculture is capital-intensive.

A factor intensity reversal occurs when the ranking of factor intensities changes as factor prices change. For example, good 1 is labor-intensive at high $w/r$ ratios but capital-intensive at low $w/r$ ratios. The H-O model typically assumes no factor intensity reversals to ensure determinate results.

Example: At high wages (relative to capital costs), firms substitute capital for labor, potentially reversing factor intensities. The H-O model assumes this does not happen within the relevant range of factor prices.

categories.description: The core prediction about trade patterns based on factor endowments.

examples.explanation:
A country will export the good that uses its abundant factor intensively and import the good that uses its scarce factor intensively. If Home is labor-abundant and manufacturing is labor-intensive, Home exports manufacturing and imports agriculture.

Example: Vietnam (labor-abundant) exports textiles (labor-intensive) and imports machinery (capital-intensive). Germany (capital-abundant) exports machinery and imports textiles.

The abundant factor is relatively cheap in autarky. The good that uses that factor intensively will have a lower autarky price in the abundant country. When trade opens, the country will export that good (sell at higher world price) and import the other good.

Example: In Vietnam, labor is cheap, so textiles (labor-intensive) are cheap. In Germany, capital is cheap, so machinery (capital-intensive) is cheap. Trade allows each country to export what it produces cheaply and import what it produces expensively.

The H-O theorem can be illustrated using production possibility frontiers (PPFs) and indifference curves. The labor-abundant country has a PPF biased toward the labor-intensive good. Autarky relative price of the labor-intensive good is lower in the labor-abundant country. Under free trade, the world price lies between the two autarky prices, and the labor-abundant country exports the labor-intensive good.

Example: Home (labor-abundant) PPF is flatter (more labor-intensive good on horizontal axis). Autarky price ratio $P_M/P_A$ is lower in Home. World price $P_M/P_A$ is higher than Home's autarky price, so Home exports manufacturing.

categories.description: How well the H-O model explains real-world trade patterns.

examples.explanation:
Wassily Leontief found that the US — the world's most capital-abundant country — exported labor-intensive goods and imported capital-intensive goods. This contradicted the H-O theorem. Leontief's input-output analysis showed that US exports had a lower capital/labor ratio than US imports.

Example: Leontief found that US exports required 13% less capital per worker than US imports. This became known as the Leontief paradox. Subsequent research resolved the paradox by including human capital (skilled labor as a form of capital) — the US is abundant in skilled labor, not just physical capital.

examples.process: Human capital and the H-O model
examples.explanation:
Incorporating human capital (education, skills, training) as a separate factor helps resolve the Leontief paradox. The US is abundant in human capital (skilled labor) and exports skill-intensive goods (software, pharmaceuticals, financial services).

Example: When labor is split into skilled and unskilled, the US is abundant in skilled labor and scarce in unskilled labor. The US exports skill-intensive goods and imports unskilled-labor-intensive goods — consistent with the H-O model.

Factor intensity reversals can undermine the H-O theorem. If factor intensities differ across countries (e.g., textiles are labor-intensive in Vietnam but capital-intensive in Germany due to different technologies), the H-O prediction may fail.

Example: Empirical evidence suggests that factor intensity reversals are rare for most goods, but they can occur for some products, especially when comparing very different countries (developed vs developing).

Modern tests of the H-O model use more sophisticated methods (gravity models, panel data) and generally find support for the model's predictions, especially when incorporating human capital, natural resources, and technology differences. However, the model explains only a portion of world trade; other factors (economies of scale, product differentiation, geography) also matter.

Example: A typical finding is that factor endowments explain about 30-50% of trade patterns, with the remainder explained by technology, scale economies, and trade costs.

worked_examples.title: Determining Trade Patterns in the Heckscher-Ohlin Model
worked_examples.problem: Two countries, Home and Foreign, produce two goods: Computers ($C$) and Textiles ($T$). Factors: Labor ($L$) and Capital ($K$). Production functions: $C = \min(L_C/2, K_C/1)$ (Leontief), $T = \min(L_T/1, K_T/2)$ (Leontief). Endowments: Home: $L=100$, $K=100$. Foreign: $L=200$, $K=50$. (a) Which country is labor-abundant? Capital-abundant? (b) Which good is labor-intensive? Capital-intensive? (c) According to the H-O theorem, what will each country export and import? (d) Calculate autarky prices (using factor prices) and confirm the trade pattern. (e) Suppose trade opens. Which good's price will rise in Home? In Foreign?

step_by_step.explanation: Home: $L/K = 100/100 = 1$. Foreign: $L/K = 200/50 = 4$. Since $4 > 1$, Foreign has a higher labor-to-capital ratio. Foreign is labor-abundant; Home is capital-abundant (or equivalently, Foreign is labor-abundant, Home is capital-abundant).
Computers: $L_C/K_C = 2/1 = 2$ (Leontief coefficients: 2 labor, 1 capital per unit). Textiles: $L_T/K_T = 1/2 = 0.5$. Since $2 > 0.5$, computers are labor-intensive; textiles are capital-intensive.

Foreign is labor-abundant and computers are labor-intensive → Foreign exports computers, imports textiles. Home is capital-abundant and textiles are capital-intensive → Home exports textiles, imports computers. This is the H-O theorem prediction.

In Foreign (labor-abundant), labor is relatively cheap, capital relatively expensive. Computers (labor-intensive) will be relatively cheap in Foreign. In Home (capital-abundant), capital is relatively cheap, labor relatively expensive. Textiles (capital-intensive) will be relatively cheap in Home. So Foreign has a lower autarky price of computers ($P_C/P_T$ lower), Home has a lower autarky price of textiles ($P_T/P_C$ lower).

When trade opens, the price of computers (the good Foreign exports) will rise in Foreign. The price of textiles (the good Home exports) will rise in Home. Conversely, the price of computers will fall in Home (import good), and the price of textiles will fall in Foreign (import good). The world price will lie between the two autarky prices.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Foreign is labor-abundant ($L/K=4 > 1$); Home is capital-abundant. (b) Computers are labor-intensive ($L/K=2$); textiles are capital-intensive ($L/K=0.5$). (c) Foreign exports computers, imports textiles; Home exports textiles, imports computers. (d) Autarky: computers cheaper in Foreign, textiles cheaper in Home. (e) Trade: $P_C$ rises in Foreign, falls in Home; $P_T$ rises in Home, falls in Foreign.
worked_examples.concept_applied: The Heckscher-Ohlin theorem predicts that countries export goods that intensively use their abundant factors. Here, labor-abundant Foreign exports labor-intensive computers; capital-abundant Home exports capital-intensive textiles. Trade aligns prices and affects factor returns (Stolper-Samuelson).

worked_examples.title: Stolper-Samuelson Theorem: Trade and Income Distribution
worked_examples.problem: Using the same setup as above: Home (capital-abundant, exports capital-intensive textiles, imports labor-intensive computers). (a) According to the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, what happens to the real wage ($w$) and real return to capital ($r$) in Home when trade opens? (b) What happens in Foreign? (c) Explain the intuition. (d) If Home is a developed country and Foreign is a developing country, what does this imply about the effects of trade on inequality in each country?

step_by_step.explanation: Home exports textiles (capital-intensive). Trade raises the relative price of textiles ($P_T/P_C$ increases). The Stolper-Samuelson theorem states that an increase in the price of a good raises the real return to the factor used intensively in that good and lowers the real return to the other factor. Textiles are capital-intensive, so the return to capital ($r$) rises. The real wage ($w$) falls. In Home (capital-abundant), trade benefits capitalists and harms workers.

Foreign exports computers (labor-intensive). Trade raises the relative price of computers ($P_C/P_T$ increases). Computers are labor-intensive, so the real wage ($w$) rises. The real return to capital ($r$) falls. In Foreign (labor-abundant), trade benefits workers and harms capitalists.

Trade raises the price of the good that uses the abundant factor intensively. This increases demand for that factor, raising its return. The other factor faces reduced demand (as production shifts away from the import-competing sector), lowering its return. In the H-O model with perfect mobility, these effects are unambiguous and opposite across factors.

In Home (developed, capital-abundant), trade benefits capital owners (the top of the income distribution) and harms workers (the bottom and middle). This tends to increase income inequality. In Foreign (developing, labor-abundant), trade benefits workers (the bottom and middle) and harms capital owners (the top). This tends to decrease income inequality. The H-O model predicts that globalization reduces inequality in developing countries and increases inequality in developed countries — a pattern broadly consistent with experience (e.g., China and India saw falling inequality in early reform periods; US and Europe saw rising inequality).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Home: $r$ rises, $w$ falls (capitalists gain, workers lose). (b) Foreign: $w$ rises, $r$ falls (workers gain, capitalists lose). (c) Trade raises price of the good using the abundant factor intensively, increasing demand for that factor. (d) Trade increases inequality in capital-abundant developed countries, decreases inequality in labor-abundant developing countries.
worked_examples.concept_applied: The Stolper-Samuelson theorem shows that trade benefits the owners of a country's abundant factor and harms owners of the scarce factor. This has profound implications for income distribution and explains why trade is often controversial — it creates clear winners and losers along class lines.

image.alt: Graph showing two countries' PPFs biased toward different goods, with autarky prices and trade equilibrium
image.caption: Heckscher-Ohlin theorem: The labor-abundant country's PPF is biased toward the labor-intensive good, giving it a lower autarky price. Trade drives prices toward equality, with each country exporting its comparative advantage good.

summary:
The Heckscher-Ohlin (H-O) model, developed by Eli Heckscher (1919) and Bertil Ohlin (1933), is a general equilibrium model of international trade that explains trade patterns based on differences in countries' factor endowments — their relative supplies of labor, capital, land, and other productive factors. Unlike the Ricardian model, which attributes trade to differences in technology (labor productivity), the H-O model assumes identical technologies across countries and explains trade by differences in factor abundance. A country will export goods that intensively use its abundant factors and import goods that intensively use its scarce factors.

The H-O model is built on several key assumptions: two countries, two goods, two factors (the $2 \times 2 \times 2$ framework); factors are perfectly mobile between sectors within a country but immobile between countries; production technologies are identical across countries but differ across goods (one good is labor-intensive, the other capital-intensive); countries differ in their factor endowments (one is labor-abundant, the other capital-abundant); preferences are identical and homothetic across countries; perfect competition prevails; and there are no trade barriers or transport costs.

The Heckscher-Ohlin theorem: A country will export the good that intensively uses its abundant factor and import the good that intensively uses its scarce factor. If Home is labor-abundant ($L/K > L^*/K^*$) and manufacturing is labor-intensive ($L_M/K_M > L_A/K_A$), then Home will export manufacturing and import agriculture. The intuition is that the abundant factor is relatively cheap, making the good that uses it intensively cheaper in autarky. Trade allows the country to export that good at higher world prices and import the other good at lower world prices.

The Stolper-Samuelson theorem: An increase in the relative price of a good raises the real return to the factor used intensively in that good and lowers the real return to the other factor. Thus, trade (which raises the price of the export good and lowers the price of the import good) benefits the owners of the abundant factor and harms the owners of the scarce factor. In a labor-abundant developing country, workers gain from trade and capitalists lose. In a capital-abundant developed country, capitalists gain and workers lose. This theorem provides a stark prediction about how globalization affects income distribution: trade tends to reduce inequality in developing countries and increase inequality in developed countries.

The Rybczynski theorem: At constant prices, an increase in the endowment of a factor increases the output of the good that uses that factor intensively and decreases the output of the other good. For example, if a labor-abundant country experiences population growth (increase in $L$), output of the labor-intensive good rises, and output of the capital-intensive good falls (at constant prices). This theorem explains how factor accumulation (population growth, capital investment, resource discovery) affects production patterns and trade over time.

The Factor Price Equalization (FPE) theorem: Under the assumptions of the H-O model, free trade leads to equalization of factor prices (wages and returns to capital) across countries, even if factors are immobile internationally. Trade in goods substitutes for factor mobility, driving factor prices to equality. Empirically, factor prices have not fully equalized due to technology differences, trade barriers, and factor immobility, but there has been some convergence, especially between developed and developing countries.

Empirical relevance and limitations: The Leontief paradox (1953) found that the US — the world's most capital-abundant country — exported labor-intensive goods and imported capital-intensive goods, contradicting the H-O theorem. Subsequent research resolved the paradox by incorporating human capital (education, skills, training) as a separate factor. The US is abundant in human capital (skilled labor) and exports skill-intensive goods (software, pharmaceuticals, financial services). When labor is split into skilled and unskilled, the H-O model performs better. Modern empirical tests generally find support for the H-O model, especially when incorporating human capital, natural resources, and technology differences, but the model explains only a portion of world trade (30-50%). Other factors — economies of scale, product differentiation, geography, trade costs, and technology differences — also matter.

Despite its limitations, the Heckscher-Ohlin model remains a cornerstone of international trade theory. It provides a powerful framework for understanding how factor endowments shape trade patterns, how trade affects income distribution (Stolper-Samuelson), how factor accumulation affects production (Rybczynski), and whether globalization leads to convergence of factor prices (FPE). The model is essential for analyzing North-South trade, the effects of globalization on inequality, and the political economy of trade policy. Understanding the H-O model is crucial for any serious student of international economics.
